steps.title: Identify two independent clauses with a contrasting relationship
steps.content: The sentence contains: (1) “Jetties can sometimes have the opposite effect” and (2) “obstructing the natural flow of sand along the shore can lead to increased erosion in some areas.” Both are complete independent clauses. “Obstructing...the shore” is a gerund phrase serving as the subject of “can lead.” The word “though” signals a contrast: jetties protect coasts, yet this same action paradoxically causes erosion. The clauses must be joined correctly.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule for transition words between independent clauses
steps.content:
When two independent clauses are connected by a concessive transition like “though,” the transition word must be set off with punctuation on both sides: a comma before it (separating it from the first clause) and punctuation after it (introducing the second clause). A semicolon after “though” properly joins two independent clauses while maintaining the contrast. The structure is: IC, though; IC—the comma isolates “though” as a transitional element, and the semicolon introduces the second independent clause. The other choices either omit necessary punctuation (C), fail to use a semicolon between independent clauses (A), or misplace the transition word (D).

That matches choice B — B is correct.
